div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 428px; height: 1px; padding-top: 313px; margin-left: 12px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; font-weight: bold; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 398px; height: 1px; padding-top: 342px; margin-left: 22px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 11px; font-family: Consolas; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div:
Inbound:
80, 443 → ALB SG, from 0.0.0.0/0
3001 → EC2 SG, from ALB SG only
5432 → RDS SG, from EC2 SG only
Outbound:
ALB → EC2 SG:3001
EC2 → RDS SG:5432, + 443 to internet
(Gemini API, SSM, ECR pull)
RDS → none (no outbound needed)

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 428px; height: 1px; padding-top: 593px; margin-left: 12px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; font-weight: bold; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 398px; height: 1px; padding-top: 622px; margin-left: 22px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 11px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div:
• EC2 is public — this build skips a NAT Gateway (~$32/mo) to stay in the AWS Free Tier; the API SG still only opens 3001 to the ALB SG, not the world.
• RDS is private — no reason a database should ever take a direct internet connection; only the EC2 SG may reach 5432.
• ALB exists — terminates HTTP today (HTTPS pending CloudFront), spans 2 AZs for availability, and decouples the public endpoint from EC2's IP (which changes on stop/start since there's no Elastic IP).
• Security Groups are stateful, layered allow-lists (ALB←internet, EC2←ALB-only, RDS←EC2-only) — a request can't skip a layer even if it knows the next hop's address.
